name: MinerU Technical Report (v1.x)
applicability: Academic paper on document layout analysis methodology. Useful for understanding the design rationale behind the pipeline backend but superseded by the 2.5 report. Not directly applicable to SoNash's current CAS work unless SoNash is building its own extraction model, which it is not.
home_connection: No direct project tie-in. Would only matter if SoNash decided to build in-house document parsing rather than consume MinerU's output.

name: MinerU 2.5 Technical Report
applicability: Covers model architecture, training strategy, data engineering, evaluation for the MinerU 2.5 VLM model. Interesting as VLM+OCR fusion prior art but deep in ML territory that SoNash is not pursuing.
home_connection: SoNash does not train models. This paper would only be useful as background reading for understanding why MinerU's VLM backend is structured the way it is.

name: OmniDocBench v1.5
applicability: The canonical benchmark for document parsing in the space (MinerU claims 86.2 on v1.5 pipeline, 90+ on VLM). Only relevant if SoNash ever needs to quantify its own extraction quality against a public benchmark — which would require SoNash to be producing extraction output, not consuming it.
home_connection: No current use case. File as reference.

name: mineru-mcp
applicability: Separate package (referenced in MinerU 3.0.0 release notes) implementing an MCP server that wraps the mineru-api HTTP API. Relevant as a reference implementation of an MCP wrapper around an HTTP service — SoNash has talked about exposing its own services as MCP tools in JASON-OS research. Worth reading the skeleton, not for direct adoption (AGPL-3.0 contagion risk).
home_connection: Reference pattern for any future SoNash MCP wrapper work. Read for shape, not copy.

name: POST /tasks async task API
applicability: This is MinerU's in-memory async task API: POST /tasks to enqueue, GET /tasks/{id} for status, GET /tasks/{id}/result for output. Four states (pending/processing/completed/failed), TTL cleanup via background loop, filesystem-backed output, in-memory dict state. Does not survive restart. Directly relevant to SoNash's 'progressive/resumable extraction' gap (Gap Agent A Q5 from T28 research). The pattern is small enough to port: the interesting part is the state-machine + semaphore-gated background worker + TTL cleanup, not any code lift.
home_connection: Gap Agent A Q5 (progressive extraction) in T28 research. Extraction candidate: knowledge-tier pattern.

name: mineru-router load balancing
applicability: Multi-GPU router with scored load balancing: score = (queued + processing + pending_assignments) / max_concurrent, with shuffle-then-stable-sort for jitter and optimistic pending_assignments increment to prevent thundering herd. Auto-restart on 2+ consecutive health failures. Separate subprocess per GPU via CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES. Relevant only if SoNash ever needs to orchestrate multiple homogeneous workers, which is not a current initiative. File as pattern for 'multi-worker orchestration' idea.
home_connection: No current SoNash use case. JASON-OS has talked about agent dispatch across multiple workers; this would be the model to revisit if that materializes.

name: Structured output schema (output_files.md)
applicability: The exemplary structured-output specification page. Specifies middle.json format: pdf_info array of per-page structures with 4-level block hierarchy (Level 1 blocks table/image → Level 2 → Lines → Spans), every field typed, every block type enumerated, bbox coordinate system documented, reading-order semantics explained, breaking changes between pipeline and VLM backends explicitly flagged. The pattern is what matters, not the schema itself. SoNash's analysis.json and synthesis output contracts could learn from this discipline — treating the output spec as a product surface.
home_connection: Directly applicable to SoNash CONVENTIONS.md §13 (handler output contract) and §17 (synthesis output contract). Extraction candidate: anti-pattern candidate against current SoNash practice of documenting schemas only in Zod — add a human-readable output contract page.

name: Environment variable catalog (cli_tools.md)
applicability: Prose catalog of 15+ MINERU_* environment variables with defaults, effects, and scoping rules. The discipline of documenting every tunable in one place is cheap and high-value. SoNash has env vars scattered across hooks, scripts, and skills with no central reference — this is a concrete anti-pattern we already have in-house.
home_connection: Extraction candidate: knowledge pattern. Apply to SoNash's hook env vars (SKIP_*, GSD_*, etc.) — create a single prose catalog mirroring cli_tools.md's layout. Could live in docs/agent_docs/ENV_VARS.md.

name: Hardware decision matrix (quick_start/index.md)
applicability: Pre-install decision matrix: 5-column table mapping parsing backend (pipeline, hybrid-auto, vlm-auto, hybrid-http-client, vlm-http-client) to 8 constraints (backend features, accuracy, OS, CPU support, GPU acceleration, min VRAM, RAM, disk, Python version). Users pick their backend before installing. Pattern applies to any multi-mode tool with real trade-offs.
home_connection: Applicable to /analyze and /media-analysis where users must choose between backends (whisper vs oEmbed vs transcript API). Extraction candidate: knowledge pattern — add a backend decision matrix to /media-analysis docs.

name: Changelog-as-capability-index (changelog.md)
applicability: Every release entry is prose with capability deltas, issue refs, CVE refs, and quantified performance improvements ('OCR speed improved 200-300%'). Breaking changes are explicitly flagged. Reading the changelog top-to-bottom gives you a feature capability index. SoNash's SESSION_HISTORY.md is thorough but optimized for session recovery, not capability discovery.
home_connection: Reference pattern for user-facing changelog discipline. Applicable if SoNash ever ships a public release with a changelog — probably not in the current scope.

name: Layered optional-dependencies install (pyproject.toml)
applicability: 8 optional-dependency groups (test, vlm, vllm, lmdeploy, mlx, pipeline, gradio, core, all) with cross-references ('core' includes vlm+pipeline+gradio, 'all' is platform-conditional). The layered pattern lets users opt into heavy deps (torch, vllm, lmdeploy) only when needed, and lets the project document the install decision via pyproject.toml itself. Applicable to any Python package with optional heavy dependencies.
home_connection: SoNash functions/package.json has no equivalent — Firebase Functions eats all deps. Not directly applicable. File as reference pattern.

name: Sliding window for long-document memory bounding
applicability: get_processing_window_size(default=64) applied identically across all three backends — pages loaded, inferred, and freed in window-sized batches. Peak memory is strictly bounded regardless of doc size. The pattern is well-known (streaming iteration) but the discipline of making it uniform across all three backends is the interesting bit.
home_connection: No SoNash equivalent need — we don't process arbitrarily large inputs. File as reference for future 'streaming analysis' patterns.

name: Keyed singleton with atexit cleanup
applicability: (backend, model_path, server_url) tuple as cache key, threading.RLock-protected, atexit shutdown, _mineru_runtime_handles for multi-path teardown probing. A clean model for expensive-to-init resources that should be shared across threads but cleaned up on interpreter exit.
home_connection: No current need. File as reference if SoNash ever needs heavy per-process caches.

notes: Three high-relevance items all tie back to existing SoNash concerns: progressive extraction gap (T28 research), output contract discipline (CONVENTIONS §13/§17), and scattered env-var config. All three become knowledge-tier extraction candidates in value-map.json. No high-relevance content candidates — the value here is architectural and documentation patterns, not portable code.
